speaker: interviewer
text: When and how did you get on the Schindler's List?

speaker: interviewee
text: As I said before, Schindler got the permission to move his factory and got the permission to make it much bigger than it was in Krakow. That means he also needed much more workers than he had. And he got the permission or he allowed himself to take 800 men and 300 women. And then began the-- this today so very famous Schindler's list. The list was made [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] through, first of all, through Schindler. And his only condition was to put on the list his workers. It means people who were coming from his factory, the 350, and a few others that he left with the time and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that he wanted them. The others were put on the list by-- everybody who saw the film or who read the book written by Keneally knows that there was a famous man who-- it was Itzhak Stern. The film shows that Itzhak Stern was making the list with the-- which ended together, which is not too accurate. By the way, if I may, I would say that there are some details in the film or in the book which are not accurate. There's no one lie. There's nothing that-- that wasn't true. And it doesn't mean too much if they mixed or, or, or related it otherwise. But if I am telling of the story so I want to make it more accurate because [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] the, the man-- one of the man who, who made the Schindler was really-- make the list was really Stern. But the main man who made the list was the man who-- who was in charge of, of so-called, I should call it manpower organization today. He was a Jewish policeman from Pleszew who was responsible for bringing the working force to the Germans when they demanded it. So he was the main man who made the list.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now how we got on the list-- You remember his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, Goldberg. Marcel Goldberg. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] How we got on the list-- through my father. How he managed it, I don't know until today. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Somebody told us that my father paid. He didn't pay. He couldn't pay because he had no money. But maybe he, he promised this man to pay him after the war if we succeed to survive. I personally believe-- we are very good friends with Stern. I personally believe-- I never asked my mother, my father. And I have no answer why I didn't ask, but it's a fact. Even it's hard to believe, it's a fact. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were very good friends with Stern. Stern knew me, was working with my father opposite to our-- to our business. And we are very good friends. He knew me since I was born. And so I think he is partially responsible-- let's call it this way-- for bringing us on the list. But we are one of the very few families which all together were on the list. There were another two or three.

speaker: interviewer
text: So who was on the list from your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother, my father, my sister, and me. So this is a very rare case because this way, all the family was saved. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And we knew that we were on the list. We didn't know how it would be-- how it would be brought to practice-- how it would be made. But it took a few days. The whole Plaszow-- sent all of the people from Plaszow. They were left only-- it was left only a group of a hundred or something people which were called liquidation comando. Among them is-- was this cousin doctor who is today's brother-in-law of mine. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we were sent-- all the men were sent to a camp which, which its name was Gross Rosen. All the women-- and were thousands-- were sent-- were sent to Auschwitz. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We spent there 10 days, which were terrible 10 days-- terrible because of the circumstance and terrible because of the conditions, terrible because of the approach to us by the Polish inmates who were sitting there as prisoners because of being murderers, thieves, and so on, and because of not getting food, because of being squeezed in, in places where 50 people had space enough to stay and we were 500 or 600. So imagine. But it, it stopped being terrible in the movement after six days, or five days, or seven days, a few days, when we knew that there is a list which each will be respected-- because we were among all these thousands who were sent away from Plaszow. And we didn't know how it-- how it would be fulfilled, the so-called list. After a few days, they put us in a special barrack that means we were separated. At that moment, we knew that we belonged to Schindler. And I don't remember exactly, but I think around 10 days, two weeks, when he stayed in Gross Rosen, we have been transported to, to Czech, to Sudety, Sudetes. And to so-called Brnenec. The place was called Brunnlitz. We-- I am speaking about the 800 men. By the way, the others, the thousands-- [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: The thousands who, who-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] were with us, were sent to two or three or four camps in Germany. They-- nobody stayed in, in Gross Rosen. And part of them perished-- until the end of the war, and it wasn't with us, after the war, part was saved, and most of them took place in-- took a part in this very famous death-- march to death. We were sent to-- we got a, a full bread, everybody of us, which, which nobody of us saw. A very long period of time-- one bread for one person. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] which afterwards, we understood it because it took a long time to go to there, although the distance was not too-- too- too big. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we went-- we, we got water. Everybody got a bottle of water or something like this. And we came-- we came to Brunnlitz. Schlindler awaited us. We-- there was a, a textile factory. And we were brought, brought to this textile factory. Afterwards, we knew that this factory was belonging to Jews before the war and was [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] taken over by Germans. And now, we were living in the same factory, in second and third floor. It was a complex of buildings. It was a very big factory, very well known. Afterwards, we laughed about it. I think the name of the owner was Behr or something like this. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The first thing we had to do is to, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to take out the machines of the textile factory, to put them away, and to put in their places the munition machines, the special machines. And by this removing of the factory-- textile factory machines, Czech workers who worked in the factory worked with us. Oh, they were demounting the machines, and we were only helping. And it's very-- I think it's important to say that we saw that this Czech workers, when demounting the machine, are paying the most attention, not to destroy anything, to demount them part of the part, to put every screw in the right place, to write down which screw belongs to what part. And then afterwards, we knew that the factory was rebuilt in months after the Germans went away by these workers. And they even found the owner was-- or one of the owners or one of the owner's children-- were here in the States. They found him, the workers, and called him to come back. I somewhere read about it. It's by the way. And then came the time of-- of-- of building the, the machines of, of, of-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] time when everything was to be prepared to make the very specific or special part of something. This was the kind of the Germans were making the parts of the ammunition. That every factory was making one part without knowing what-- so they said this is the safest way.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we made the-- we had to make also some part. I, I remember, I recall that this was called MU5 or something like this. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- after a while, after two months or one month or two months, most of the machines were ready. Everybody got his job to work on some machine. I-- I got-- I, I was working on the small machine. And we have to begin to do something.

speaker: interviewer
text: To produce.

speaker: interviewee
text: To produce. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In order to, to say now what should be said at the end, we didn't produce anything. During all the months when we stayed there, we didn't produce anything. I don't think that there were two or three or four boxes which went out of the-- out of this factory. We know that he was asked, after a few months, he was asked to send samples [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] what he's doing or how he's doing.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mean Schindler was asked?

speaker: interviewee
text: Schindler. So he bought somewhere. There was another factory who made the same. He bought it and sent.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why didn't you produce?

speaker: interviewee
text: First of all, he didn't, he didn't ask us-- I don't know. We didn't work. We worked-- we worked on-- in order not to work. We, we, oh, we worked in order not to produce. This-- in my machine, you know, there were two boxes from the left side and two boxes from the side-- right side. From-- left side was unmade part. And the-- from the right, I had to put-- it was always half full, this and this. All the time. Who filled it, I don't know unto today. Maybe we made some. We put and then we again. It was-- we didn't really produce. In, in the reality, we didn't produce anything. He didn't pay any-- I think he wasn't interested in, in-- he was bluffing. He was bluffing.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Germans.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he didn't ask us to-- nothing. On the contrary, there were times when the inspection was coming. People were coming from, from the high places from the capital or somewhere to inspect. At that time, he made ourselves working hard-- looking working very hard. But he, he was always doing the same trick, usually very well known. The commissioner which came, they were always very high-positioned people, all very high-positioned officers, army officers or something like this. So he took them to his office, kept them for a few hours. They came out, all of them drunk, but not Schindler. So when they came down to see us, they didn't see us like they should see us. And this was done, and it took place many times.

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to take you back a little bit. You said that the 800 men came to Brunnlitz. What happened to the women?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, this is the most dramatic part of Schindler's story. Like we went to, to Gross Rosen, the older women were sent to Auschwitz. And these [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] women were sent, like we, together with thousands of others from Plaszow. And in-- when they arrived in Auschwitz, what was Auschwitz, everybody knows, even you at the time, the commanding officer of Auschwitz, maybe he knew that he can get something from Schindler. And maybe that he was just a devil like he was, he told, almost immediately, he let Schindler know he's accepting the order from Berlin that 300 women have, have to be brought to Brunnlitz to, to Schlindler. But he insists that the 300 women he will decide who they are and not Schindler. And then began their big struggle because Schindler told officially or wrote officially or was talking to Berlin that these are the most capable, professional, and whatever you say for, for his purposes and his factory. He cannot accept just 300-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] not-- not-- not the right women he wants. So he needs to snow them, and he didn't succeed. I don't remember exactly, but it took some weeks. And we got panicked with the time because every day which-- when it was late and the women didn't arrived, we were beginning to lose our hopes because, you know, knowing that there were in Auschwitz, we knew what, what may happen in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we began-- 'we,' that means 'the people who were around, surrounding Schindler who were-- who were able to influence Schindler, whom he also believed and wanted them-- he wanted them to influence him.' So these people began to, to [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] act and to press on, to make pressure on Schindler. He should act. Were these the leaders of the survivors? These were the Jewish leaders?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, these were, these were a leader group from our--

speaker: interviewer
text: From your list.

speaker: interviewee
text: From our list, yeah. Four or five people. But the main man was Stern. He was the most influential, and he was the man who was kind of real adviser. And we know now-- now we know for sure but we knew even at that time-- that Schindler doesn't make anything without at least letting know Stern and mostly taking advice from Schindler.

speaker: interviewer
text: From Stern.

speaker: interviewee
text: From Stern. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So he began to, to, to, to ask him to act. And at that time, he, again, what we see of what we saw in the film that Schindler went to Auschwitz and gave diamonds to the commanding officer, this is not the right-- this is not accurate, let's say it this way, because he really bribed them and really settled it but his way. He sent his mistress to, to Auschwitz. And you may imagine that this mistress was a beauty, a real beauty. And he told her he would know it positively. He told-- he gave her diamonds and told her you, you, you do what you can. You give the diamonds when you have to do-- to give them, and you use your body if you need this. You-- that means--

speaker: interviewer
text: It was that important for him to bring the women back.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And we don't know what happened there, but the diamonds were given. After she went there, she brought the women. She came with the women. So just again, there are some details in the-- in the film or in the book-- but the book, it's written I think the right way-- which I'm not sure it's correct, but it doesn't matter for somebody who wasn't there. It's not real lies, a little bit changed. Some of the women came. All of the women--

speaker: interviewer
text: Among them, your sister and mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Among them my sister and my mother. And again, this is a way we were saved all our family, and we survived, all our family. And there's another detail if I may say that I was saved-- I always say that I was saved twice by Schindler. The also story which was written about in the book that we say we are always-- all saved by Schindler because we were on Schindler's list. And I will say-- I say that I was saved twice because once I was accused on sabotage, which meant death, and Schindler saved me personally. That means that I was saved twice.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you explain what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: At the time when they, they were arranging the machines, there was one machine where some very special part had to be made for the special-- for this special machine. This was all automatic. And they didn't succeed to make this part. They always failed. There were engineers working on it, German engineers belonging to Schindler, not prisoners. They didn't succeed. It took them a few times to, to, to try it on, and it didn't go. At last, they said, this is the right part, and now it works. And as lucky as I was, I was the man who was chosen to activate the machine as the first one. And the whole thing I had to do is to put the small part which we were producing-- I don't know it's what's the small part of steel. I don't know exactly what it is-- to put it, it into such a gap and to push a button. That was all.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I pushed the button, and the whole part broke. The part that which they were making many times and didn't succeed. So there-- it was something very terrible for them because until they succeeded to say or to make it the right way, and I was the cause that it broke. I don't know if I broke it because the whole thing I was-- the only thing I did was to push the button. But maybe I put-- I put it in a wrong way. It was broken. So they had to make it again and again and so on. So the engineers thought I was sabotaging. They told whom?

speaker: interviewee
text: Amongst themselves, they make a, a, a big cry. And don't forget that the Brunnlitz camp was, as I told you, if you made it to Gross Rosen, owned by Schindler, but conducted by SS, by Germans. And we had a commanding officer who was something-- a very terrible man. His name was Leipold. He was one of-- of the-- not one. He was the one who, who exterminated a full village next to Lublin in Poland, two years ago, three years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the engineers-- it was a day when Schindler wasn't in the camp-- in the factory. And the engineers went to Leipold and told him, listen, there's a sabotager.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there were rules. If somebody is sabotage, he has to be-- it has to be reported to this and this places, and the man has to be transported to the main camp. And this was a death sentence because if, if you are a saboteur and if you are reported, if you are sent, right, you can only goes to-- go to gas chamber or to be shot or hanged or something like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: So at that time, Schindler wasn't there, but we succeeded to postpone sending the reports [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] because in the office where the reports had to be written were Jews, were prisoners working. And on the other day or in two days, Schindler came. And again, Stern and-- mostly Stern approached him and said, listen, this and this happened, and this young man is going to be sent back. So he understood that he has to act. And he went to Leipold, to this commanding officer, and told him, listen, I know what happened. I am very furious, and I am-- OK, let-- let's-- let's stop.
